tools.name: configuration_view
tools.description: Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML

minified.description: Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)

tools.name: events_list
tools.description: List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter events by field values (e.g. 'type=Warning', 'involvedObject.name=my-pod'). Supported fields: involvedObject.kind, involvedObject.name, involvedObject.namespace, involvedObject.uid, involvedObject.apiVersion, involvedObject.resourceVersion, involvedObject.fieldPath, reason, reportingComponent, source, type. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces

tools.name: namespaces_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter namespaces by field values (e.g. 'metadata.name=default', 'status.phase=Active'). Supported fields: metadata.name, status.phase. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

tools.name: nodes_log
tools.description: Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet

name.description: Name of the node to get logs from

query.description: query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")

tailLines.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)

tools.name: nodes_stats_summary
tools.description: Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics

name.description: Name of the node to get stats from

tools.name: nodes_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)

tools.name: pods_delete
tools.description: Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

name.description: Name of the Pod to delete

namespace.description: Namespace to delete the Pod from

tools.name: pods_exec
tools.description: Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command

command.description: Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]

container.description: Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod where the command will be executed

namespace.description: Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed

tools.name: pods_get
tools.description: Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod from

tools.name: pods_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_list_in_namespace
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the specified namespace in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_log
tools.description: Get the logs of a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

container.description: Name of the Pod container to get the logs from (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the logs from

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod logs from

tail.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, default: 100)

tools.name: pods_run
tools.description: Run a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided container image and optional name

image.description: Container Image to run in the Pod

name.description: Name of the Pod (Optional, random name if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to run the Pod in

port.description: TCP/IP port to expose from the Pod container (Optional, no port exposed if not provided)

tools.name: pods_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Pods in the all namespaces, the provided namespace, or the current namespace

all_namespaces.description: If true, list the resource consumption for all Pods in all namespaces. If false, list the resource consumption for Pods in the provided namespace or the current namespace

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Pods in the namespace if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pods resource consumption from (Optional, current namespace if not provided and all_namespaces is false)

tools.name: resources_create_or_update
tools.description:
Create or update a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing a YAML or JSON representation of the resource
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

resource.description: A JSON or YAML containing a representation of the Kubernetes resource. Should include top-level fields such as apiVersion,kind,metadata, and spec

tools.name: resources_delete
tools.description:
Delete a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

gracePeriodSeconds.description: Optional duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. Value must be non-negative integer. The value zero indicates delete immediately. If this value is nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to delete the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will delete resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_get
tools.description:
Get a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_list
tools.description:
List Kubernetes resources and objects in the current cluster by providing their apiVersion and kind and optionally the namespace and label selector
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resources (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter resources by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'metadata.name=myresource'). Supported fields vary by resource type. For Pods: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

kind.description: kind of the resources (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the resources by label

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resources from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will list resources from all namespaces

tools.name: resources_scale
tools.description: Get or update the scale of a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, name, and optionally the namespace. If the scale is set in the tool call, the scale will be updated to that value. Always returns the current scale of the resource

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are apps/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: StatefulSet, Deployment)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to get/update the namespaced resource scale from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get/update resource scale from configured namespace

scale.description: Optional scale to update the resources scale to. If not provided, will return the current scale of the resource, and not update it

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I deployed and changed:
- Canary Deployment: engine-v2-1 (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - replicas: 2
  - labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - container: rec-engine
  - containerPort: 80
  - security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext)
- Service update: recommendation-engine (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - selector updated to app=recommendation-engine (removed version=v2.0) so it targets both stable and canary
  - targetPort remains 8080 (same as before)

Verification:
- Pods
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- Endpoints for Service now include 4 pod IPs (2 from v2.0 and 2 from v2.1), so traffic will be roughly 50/50 by round‑robin.

Notes and rollbacks:
- Roll back routing to stable only: set Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0 or delete/scale down the canary (kubectl scale deploy/engine-v2-1 -n canary-deployment-ns --replicas=0).
- For precise weighted splits beyond replica-based balancing, consider two Services with an Ingress/TrafficSplit/Service Mesh.

Want me to watch logs/metrics for the canary or pin the Service back if error rates rise?

Steps.message:
namespace/canary-deployment-ns created
deployment.apps/engine-v2-0 created
service/recommendation-engine created

Done.

What I deployed and changed:
- Canary Deployment: engine-v2-1 (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - replicas: 2
  - labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - container: rec-engine
  - containerPort: 80
  - security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext)
- Service update: recommendation-engine (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - selector updated to app=recommendation-engine (removed version=v2.0) so it targets both stable and canary
  - targetPort remains 8080 (same as before)

Verification:
- Pods
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- Endpoints for Service now include 4 pod IPs (2 from v2.0 and 2 from v2.1), so traffic will be roughly 50/50 by round‑robin.

Notes and rollbacks:
- Roll back routing to stable only: set Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0 or delete/scale down the canary (kubectl scale deploy/engine-v2-1 -n canary-deployment-ns --replicas=0).
- For precise weighted splits beyond replica-based balancing, consider two Services with an Ingress/TrafficSplit/Service Mesh.

Want me to watch logs/metrics for the canary or pin the Service back if error rates rise?

outputSteps.content:
Done.

What I deployed and changed:
- Canary Deployment: engine-v2-1 (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - replicas: 2
  - labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - container: rec-engine
  - containerPort: 80
  - security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext)
- Service update: recommendation-engine (namespace: canary-deployment-ns)
  - selector updated to app=recommendation-engine (removed version=v2.0) so it targets both stable and canary
  - targetPort remains 8080 (same as before)

Verification:
- Pods
  - engine-v2-0: 2/2 Running
  - engine-v2-1: 2/2 Running
- Endpoints for Service now include 4 pod IPs (2 from v2.0 and 2 from v2.1), so traffic will be roughly 50/50 by round‑robin.

Notes and rollbacks:
- Roll back routing to stable only: set Service selector back to app=recommendation-engine,version=v2.0 or delete/scale down the canary (kubectl scale deploy/engine-v2-1 -n canary-deployment-ns --replicas=0).
- For precise weighted splits beyond replica-based balancing, consider two Services with an Ingress/TrafficSplit/Service Mesh.

Want me to watch logs/metrics for the canary or pin the Service back if error rates rise?

results.taskOutput:
Short answer: nothing is wrong with the pod itself.

- Pod status: Running and Ready; no warnings/errors in events. Container has 0 restarts.
- Logs show intermittent “division by zero” errors because the app code intentionally does this 25% of the time.
- The code comes from the mounted ConfigMap calc-app-map. It runs:
  - if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0: result = 1 / 0 (caught and logged as “failed with error: division by zero”)
  - sleep_time grows as (1.2**counter)/20, so logs will slow down over time.

If you don’t want failures:
- Edit the ConfigMap to remove the 1/0 branch (e.g., compute a safe result or just log a simulated failure without raising).
- Then restart the pod to pick up the updated script (ConfigMap changes won’t affect the already-running Python process).
results.taskJudgeReason: Pass under CONTAINS: The MODEL_RESPONSE explicitly states that logs show intermittent “division by zero” errors and even references code performing 1/0, which semantically includes the core information in the REFERENCE_ANSWER (“division by zero”). Extra contextual details do not contradict and are permitted.

apiVersion: v1
data:
  calc-app.py: |
    import random
    import sys
    import time

    print("Starting calc-app...")
    sys.stdout.flush()

    counter = 0
    while True:
        counter += 1
        if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:
            try:
                result = 1 / 0
            except ZeroDivisionError as e:
                print(f"Run {counter} failed with error: {e}")
                sys.stdout.flush()
        else:
            print(f"Run {counter} succeeded")
            sys.stdout.flush()

        sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20
        time.sleep(sleep_time)

{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"apiVersion: v1
data:
  calc-app.py: |
    import random
    import sys
    import time

    print("Starting calc-app...")
    sys.stdout.flush()

    counter = 0
    while True:
        counter += 1
        if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:
            try:
                result = 1 / 0
            except ZeroDivisionError as e:
                print(f"Run {counter} failed with error: {e}")
                sys.stdout.flush()
        else:
            print(f"Run {counter} succeeded")
            sys.stdout.flush()

        sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20
        time.sleep(sleep_time)

outputSteps.content:
Short answer: nothing is wrong with the pod itself.

- Pod status: Running and Ready; no warnings/errors in events. Container has 0 restarts.
- Logs show intermittent “division by zero” errors because the app code intentionally does this 25% of the time.
- The code comes from the mounted ConfigMap calc-app-map. It runs:
  - if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0: result = 1 / 0 (caught and logged as “failed with error: division by zero”)
  - sleep_time grows as (1.2**counter)/20, so logs will slow down over time.

If you don’t want failures:
- Edit the ConfigMap to remove the 1/0 branch (e.g., compute a safe result or just log a simulated failure without raising).
- Then restart the pod to pick up the updated script (ConfigMap changes won’t affect the already-running Python process).

Steps.message: Pass under CONTAINS: The MODEL_RESPONSE explicitly states that logs show intermittent “division by zero” errors and even references code performing 1/0, which semantically includes the core information in the REFERENCE_ANSWER (“division by zero”). Extra contextual details do not contradict and are permitted.

- Root cause: The Deployment overrode the container command with /bin/sh -c and args: nonexistent_command, causing the pod to exit immediately and CrashLoop.
- Fix applied: Updated the Deployment crashloop-test/app to start NGINX in the foreground:
  - command: ["/bin/sh","-c"]
  - args: ["nginx -g 'daemon off;' "]
- Current state: A new ReplicaSet was created and the pod is healthy.
  - Pod: app-64dc8d87b5-wb56j
  - Status: 1/1 Running
  - Namespace: crashloop-test

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the command/args override entirely—just say the word.

  name: app
  namespace: crashloop-test
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:

        command: ["/bin/sh","-c"]
        args: ["nginx -g 'daemon off;' "]

Steps.message:
Done.

- Root cause: The Deployment overrode the container command with /bin/sh -c and args: nonexistent_command, causing the pod to exit immediately and CrashLoop.
- Fix applied: Updated the Deployment crashloop-test/app to start NGINX in the foreground:
  - command: ["/bin/sh","-c"]
  - args: ["nginx -g 'daemon off;' "]
- Current state: A new ReplicaSet was created and the pod is healthy.
  - Pod: app-64dc8d87b5-wb56j
  - Status: 1/1 Running
  - Namespace: crashloop-test

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the command/args override entirely—just say the word.

outputSteps.content:
Done.

- Root cause: The Deployment overrode the container command with /bin/sh -c and args: nonexistent_command, causing the pod to exit immediately and CrashLoop.
- Fix applied: Updated the Deployment crashloop-test/app to start NGINX in the foreground:
  - command: ["/bin/sh","-c"]
  - args: ["nginx -g 'daemon off;' "]
- Current state: A new ReplicaSet was created and the pod is healthy.
  - Pod: app-64dc8d87b5-wb56j
  - Status: 1/1 Running
  - Namespace: crashloop-test

If you prefer to rely on the image’s default entrypoint instead, I can remove the command/args override entirely—just say the word.

results.taskOutput:
I found the Deployment failing due to an invalid image reference/tag and then an unauthorized pull:

- Original image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag → ImagePullBackOff (not found)
- First fix attempt (quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable) → 401 UNAUTHORIZED from quay.io

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment to use a public image that does not require authentication:
  image: nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable

Current status
- app Deployment (namespace debug) is Available with 1/1 ready replica.
- Pod app-cdd766799-tnkcb is Running.

If you prefer to keep using quay.io, I can configure an imagePullSecret for your registry credentials. I can also pin the image by digest and add liveness/readiness probes if you’d like.

Steps.message:
I found the Deployment failing due to an invalid image reference/tag and then an unauthorized pull:

- Original image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag → ImagePullBackOff (not found)
- First fix attempt (quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable) → 401 UNAUTHORIZED from quay.io

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment to use a public image that does not require authentication:
  image: nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable

Current status
- app Deployment (namespace debug) is Available with 1/1 ready replica.
- Pod app-cdd766799-tnkcb is Running.

If you prefer to keep using quay.io, I can configure an imagePullSecret for your registry credentials. I can also pin the image by digest and add liveness/readiness probes if you’d like.

outputSteps.content:
I found the Deployment failing due to an invalid image reference/tag and then an unauthorized pull:

- Original image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag → ImagePullBackOff (not found)
- First fix attempt (quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable) → 401 UNAUTHORIZED from quay.io

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment to use a public image that does not require authentication:
  image: nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable

Current status
- app Deployment (namespace debug) is Available with 1/1 ready replica.
- Pod app-cdd766799-tnkcb is Running.

If you prefer to keep using quay.io, I can configure an imagePullSecret for your registry credentials. I can also pin the image by digest and add liveness/readiness probes if you’d like.

Steps.message:
deployment.apps/app condition met
Pod is stable. Verification successful.

results.taskOutput:
Fixed. homepage-pod is now Running and Ready in homepage-ns.

What was wrong
- The pod used PVC homepage-pvc with storageClassName: sc, but no StorageClass named sc existed. This left the PVC unbound, so the pod couldn’t schedule.

What I changed
- Deleted the pending PVC homepage-pvc.
- Recreated it with storageClassName: standard (available in your cluster, provisioned by rancher.io/local-path).
- The PVC bound successfully and the pod scheduled, pulled the image, and started.

Current state
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi, RWO)
- Pod homepage-pod: Running, Ready, ContainersReady

How to verify
- kubectl get pod homepage-pod -n homepage-ns -o wide
- kubectl get pvc -n homepage-ns

Note
- If you intended a different storage backend, create a StorageClass named sc or update the PVC to the desired StorageClass.

Steps.message:
namespace/homepage-ns created
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc created
pod/homepage-pod created

Fixed. homepage-pod is now Running and Ready in homepage-ns.

What was wrong
- The pod used PVC homepage-pvc with storageClassName: sc, but no StorageClass named sc existed. This left the PVC unbound, so the pod couldn’t schedule.

What I changed
- Deleted the pending PVC homepage-pvc.
- Recreated it with storageClassName: standard (available in your cluster, provisioned by rancher.io/local-path).
- The PVC bound successfully and the pod scheduled, pulled the image, and started.

Current state
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi, RWO)
- Pod homepage-pod: Running, Ready, ContainersReady

How to verify
- kubectl get pod homepage-pod -n homepage-ns -o wide
- kubectl get pvc -n homepage-ns

Note
- If you intended a different storage backend, create a StorageClass named sc or update the PVC to the desired StorageClass.

outputSteps.content:
Fixed. homepage-pod is now Running and Ready in homepage-ns.

What was wrong
- The pod used PVC homepage-pvc with storageClassName: sc, but no StorageClass named sc existed. This left the PVC unbound, so the pod couldn’t schedule.

What I changed
- Deleted the pending PVC homepage-pvc.
- Recreated it with storageClassName: standard (available in your cluster, provisioned by rancher.io/local-path).
- The PVC bound successfully and the pod scheduled, pulled the image, and started.

Current state
- PVC homepage-pvc: Bound (1Gi, RWO)
- Pod homepage-pod: Running, Ready, ContainersReady

How to verify
- kubectl get pod homepage-pod -n homepage-ns -o wide
- kubectl get pvc -n homepage-ns

Note
- If you intended a different storage backend, create a StorageClass named sc or update the PVC to the desired StorageClass.

Steps.message:

ℹWaiting for PVC 'homepage-pvc' to be 'Bound'...

Waiting for Pod 'homepage-pod' to be 'Ready'...

results.taskOutput:
I found and fixed two misconfigurations that were keeping your webapp unready in the orders namespace.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the nginx-unprivileged image, which listens on port 8080, but the probes and Service targeted port 80.
- The liveness/readiness probes were checking non-existent paths (/get_status and /is_ready).

What I changed
- Updated Deployment orders/webapp:
  - Probes now hit path / on port 8080.
  - Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated Service orders/webapp:
  - targetPort set to 8080 (Service still on port 80 inside the cluster).

Current status
- Pod is healthy and Ready: webapp-77dd4b96b5-bqg8x 1/1 Running.
- Endpoints are healthy and point to the pod on port 8080.

How to verify/access
- In-cluster: curl http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local
- From your machine: kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you want external access (Ingress/LoadBalancer), tell me your preferred method and hostname, and I’ll set it up. Additionally, I recommend pinning the image to a specific tag (not latest) and adding resource requests/limits—happy to update those too.

Steps.message:
namespace/orders created
deployment.apps/webapp created
service/webapp created
Waiting for pod to start and begin failing health checks...
deployment.apps/webapp condition met
Setup successful: Deployment is no longer available.

I found and fixed two misconfigurations that were keeping your webapp unready in the orders namespace.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the nginx-unprivileged image, which listens on port 8080, but the probes and Service targeted port 80.
- The liveness/readiness probes were checking non-existent paths (/get_status and /is_ready).

What I changed
- Updated Deployment orders/webapp:
  - Probes now hit path / on port 8080.
  - Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated Service orders/webapp:
  - targetPort set to 8080 (Service still on port 80 inside the cluster).

Current status
- Pod is healthy and Ready: webapp-77dd4b96b5-bqg8x 1/1 Running.
- Endpoints are healthy and point to the pod on port 8080.

How to verify/access
- In-cluster: curl http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local
- From your machine: kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you want external access (Ingress/LoadBalancer), tell me your preferred method and hostname, and I’ll set it up. Additionally, I recommend pinning the image to a specific tag (not latest) and adding resource requests/limits—happy to update those too.

outputSteps.content:
I found and fixed two misconfigurations that were keeping your webapp unready in the orders namespace.

What was wrong
- The Deployment used the nginx-unprivileged image, which listens on port 8080, but the probes and Service targeted port 80.
- The liveness/readiness probes were checking non-existent paths (/get_status and /is_ready).

What I changed
- Updated Deployment orders/webapp:
  - Probes now hit path / on port 8080.
  - Exposed container port 8080.
- Updated Service orders/webapp:
  - targetPort set to 8080 (Service still on port 80 inside the cluster).

Current status
- Pod is healthy and Ready: webapp-77dd4b96b5-bqg8x 1/1 Running.
- Endpoints are healthy and point to the pod on port 8080.

How to verify/access
- In-cluster: curl http://webapp.orders.svc.cluster.local
- From your machine: kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you want external access (Ingress/LoadBalancer), tell me your preferred method and hostname, and I’ll set it up. Additionally, I recommend pinning the image to a specific tag (not latest) and adding resource requests/limits—happy to update those too.

Steps.message:
Checking if the pod is running and ready...
pod/webapp-77dd4b96b5-bqg8x condition met
Success: Pod is now Ready
Current liveness probe path: /
Current readiness probe path: /
Success: Both probe paths have been fixed
Success: Pod is stable with acceptable number of restarts

results.taskOutput:
Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role only allowed listing deployments in the apps API group, not pods. So the pod-reader ServiceAccount had no permission to list pods in simple-rbac-setup.

What I changed
- Updated the Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup to grant pods permissions in the core API group:
  - apiGroups: [""]
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- The existing RoleBinding pod-reader-binding already binds this Role to the ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader, so no change was needed there.

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup list pods
  - Expected: yes
- Then try: kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup get pods

Notes
- This grants access only within the simple-rbac-setup namespace. If you need to list pods cluster-wide, use a ClusterRole with a ClusterRoleBinding instead.

arguments.resource:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader-role
  namespace: simple-rbac-setup
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

Steps.message:
namespace/simple-rbac-setup created
serviceaccount/pod-reader created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-role created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-binding created

Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role only allowed listing deployments in the apps API group, not pods. So the pod-reader ServiceAccount had no permission to list pods in simple-rbac-setup.

What I changed
- Updated the Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup to grant pods permissions in the core API group:
  - apiGroups: [""]
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- The existing RoleBinding pod-reader-binding already binds this Role to the ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader, so no change was needed there.

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup list pods
  - Expected: yes
- Then try: kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup get pods

Notes
- This grants access only within the simple-rbac-setup namespace. If you need to list pods cluster-wide, use a ClusterRole with a ClusterRoleBinding instead.

outputSteps.content:
Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role only allowed listing deployments in the apps API group, not pods. So the pod-reader ServiceAccount had no permission to list pods in simple-rbac-setup.

What I changed
- Updated the Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup to grant pods permissions in the core API group:
  - apiGroups: [""]
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- The existing RoleBinding pod-reader-binding already binds this Role to the ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader, so no change was needed there.

How to verify
- kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup list pods
  - Expected: yes
- Then try: kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup get pods

Notes
- This grants access only within the simple-rbac-setup namespace. If you need to list pods cluster-wide, use a ClusterRole with a ClusterRoleBinding instead.

Steps.message: Verification successful: RBAC role correctly reconfigured.

results.taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- The nginx Deployment’s pods are labeled app=nginx and the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080.
- The Service web/nginx was misconfigured:
  - Selector used app=web (didn’t match any pods), so it had no endpoints.
  - targetPort was 80 (but the container listens on 8080).

Fix applied
- Updated Service web/nginx:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports[0].targetPort: 8080
- Result: the Service now points to the running pod. Endpoints show 10.244.0.34:8080 for svc web/nginx, and the ClusterIP is 10.96.227.105:80.

How to verify (in-cluster)
- From any pod: curl http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local or curl http://10.96.227.105
- From your machine via port-forward: kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80 then curl http://localhost:8080

Note on external access
- The Service is type ClusterIP (in-cluster only). If you need access from outside the cluster, either:
  - Change it to NodePort (quick and simple), or
  - Create an Ingress (recommended with an ingress controller).
Say the word and I can set up either option.

web         v1           Pod    web-net-debug           0/1     Completed   1 (13s ago)   14s   10.244.0.35   mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane   <none>           <none>            app.kubernetes.io/component=web-net-debug,app.kubernetes.io/managed-by=kubernetes-mcp-server,app.kubernetes.io/name=web-net-debug,app.kubernetes.io/part-of=kubernetes-mcp-server-run-sandbox

Steps.message:
namespace/web created
deployment.apps/nginx created
service/nginx created

Diagnosis
- The nginx Deployment’s pods are labeled app=nginx and the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080.
- The Service web/nginx was misconfigured:
  - Selector used app=web (didn’t match any pods), so it had no endpoints.
  - targetPort was 80 (but the container listens on 8080).

Fix applied
- Updated Service web/nginx:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports[0].targetPort: 8080
- Result: the Service now points to the running pod. Endpoints show 10.244.0.34:8080 for svc web/nginx, and the ClusterIP is 10.96.227.105:80.

How to verify (in-cluster)
- From any pod: curl http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local or curl http://10.96.227.105
- From your machine via port-forward: kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80 then curl http://localhost:8080

Note on external access
- The Service is type ClusterIP (in-cluster only). If you need access from outside the cluster, either:
  - Change it to NodePort (quick and simple), or
  - Create an Ingress (recommended with an ingress controller).
Say the word and I can set up either option.

outputSteps.content:
Diagnosis
- The nginx Deployment’s pods are labeled app=nginx and the image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080.
- The Service web/nginx was misconfigured:
  - Selector used app=web (didn’t match any pods), so it had no endpoints.
  - targetPort was 80 (but the container listens on 8080).

Fix applied
- Updated Service web/nginx:
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports[0].targetPort: 8080
- Result: the Service now points to the running pod. Endpoints show 10.244.0.34:8080 for svc web/nginx, and the ClusterIP is 10.96.227.105:80.

How to verify (in-cluster)
- From any pod: curl http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local or curl http://10.96.227.105
- From your machine via port-forward: kubectl -n web port-forward svc/nginx 8080:80 then curl http://localhost:8080

Note on external access
- The Service is type ClusterIP (in-cluster only). If you need access from outside the cluster, either:
  - Change it to NodePort (quick and simple), or
  - Create an Ingress (recommended with an ingress controller).
Say the word and I can set up either option.

If you see this page, nginx is successfully installed and working. Further configuration is required for the web server, reverse proxy, API gateway, load balancer, content cache, or other features.

pod "test-connection" deleted from web namespace

results.taskOutput:
Root cause
- Your Deployment had a nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu, but no node in the cluster has that label. The scheduler could not place the pods and reported “0/1 nodes didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector,” so the Service had no endpoints. That’s why clients saw “connection refused.”

What I changed
- Removed the nodeSelector from the web-app-deployment so it can schedule on the available node.
- Result:
  - 2 pods are now Running in webshop-frontend.
  - Service web-app-service has healthy endpoints on port 80.

Current status
- Pods: 2/2 Running
- Service endpoints: present (backed by the two pod IPs)
- The connection errors should clear as clients retry.

How to verify
- From your workstation: kubectl -n webshop-frontend get pods,svc,endpoints
- In-cluster check (optional):
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run tmp-curl --rm -it --image=curlimages/curl -- curl -sS web-app-service
  - Expect to see the NGINX welcome page output.

If you intended to pin these pods to specific nodes
- Either re-add nodeSelector after labeling a node appropriately:
  - kubectl label node <your-node> environment=production-gpu
- Or use a different label that your nodes already have.

Tell me if you want me to reintroduce node placement constraints with the correct labels/tolerations.

Steps.message:
namespace/webshop-frontend created
service/web-app-service created
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment created
Waiting for resources to be created...
deployment.apps/web-app-deployment condition met
Warning: v1 Endpoints is deprecated in v1.33+; use discovery.k8s.io/v1 EndpointSlice
Setup successful: Service has no endpoints as expected

Root cause
- Your Deployment had a nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu, but no node in the cluster has that label. The scheduler could not place the pods and reported “0/1 nodes didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector,” so the Service had no endpoints. That’s why clients saw “connection refused.”

What I changed
- Removed the nodeSelector from the web-app-deployment so it can schedule on the available node.
- Result:
  - 2 pods are now Running in webshop-frontend.
  - Service web-app-service has healthy endpoints on port 80.

Current status
- Pods: 2/2 Running
- Service endpoints: present (backed by the two pod IPs)
- The connection errors should clear as clients retry.

How to verify
- From your workstation: kubectl -n webshop-frontend get pods,svc,endpoints
- In-cluster check (optional):
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run tmp-curl --rm -it --image=curlimages/curl -- curl -sS web-app-service
  - Expect to see the NGINX welcome page output.

If you intended to pin these pods to specific nodes
- Either re-add nodeSelector after labeling a node appropriately:
  - kubectl label node <your-node> environment=production-gpu
- Or use a different label that your nodes already have.

Tell me if you want me to reintroduce node placement constraints with the correct labels/tolerations.

outputSteps.content:
Root cause
- Your Deployment had a nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu, but no node in the cluster has that label. The scheduler could not place the pods and reported “0/1 nodes didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector,” so the Service had no endpoints. That’s why clients saw “connection refused.”

What I changed
- Removed the nodeSelector from the web-app-deployment so it can schedule on the available node.
- Result:
  - 2 pods are now Running in webshop-frontend.
  - Service web-app-service has healthy endpoints on port 80.

Current status
- Pods: 2/2 Running
- Service endpoints: present (backed by the two pod IPs)
- The connection errors should clear as clients retry.

How to verify
- From your workstation: kubectl -n webshop-frontend get pods,svc,endpoints
- In-cluster check (optional):
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run tmp-curl --rm -it --image=curlimages/curl -- curl -sS web-app-service
  - Expect to see the NGINX welcome page output.

If you intended to pin these pods to specific nodes
- Either re-add nodeSelector after labeling a node appropriately:
  - kubectl label node <your-node> environment=production-gpu
- Or use a different label that your nodes already have.

Tell me if you want me to reintroduce node placement constraints with the correct labels/tolerations.

  - name: web-server

    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
      name: http
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 2
      periodSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 3
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
  - name: logger
    image: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
  volumes:
  - name: logs-volume
    emptyDir: {}

results.taskOutput:
I updated the PVC to request 3Gi and enabled volume expansion on the StorageClass:

- Set allowVolumeExpansion: true on StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path)
- Updated PersistentVolumeClaim resize-pv/storage-pvc to requests.storage: 3Gi

Current state:
- PVC spec requests: 3Gi
- PVC status capacity: 1Gi (still)
- Events show: “waiting for an external controller to expand this PVC”

Note:
- The local-path provisioner (rancher.io/local-path) does not provide a CSI external-resizer, so the capacity reported on the PV/PVC is unlikely to update despite the requested size being 3Gi. The pod mount is a hostPath-backed directory and won’t “grow” like a block device.

If you need an actually expanded filesystem with reflected capacity, use a CSI storage class that supports expansion (with an external-resizer) and migrate the PVC, or I can set that up for you.

NAMESPACE            APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                      READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   IP           NODE                             NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS
local-path-storage   v1           Pod    local-path-provisioner-7b8c8ddbd6-gwb2b   1/1     Running   0          30m   10.244.0.2   mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane   <none>           <none>            app=local-path-provisioner,pod-template-hash=7b8c8ddbd6

Steps.message:
namespace/resize-pv created
persistentvolumeclaim/storage-pvc created
pod/storage-pod created

I updated the PVC to request 3Gi and enabled volume expansion on the StorageClass:

- Set allowVolumeExpansion: true on StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path)
- Updated PersistentVolumeClaim resize-pv/storage-pvc to requests.storage: 3Gi

Current state:
- PVC spec requests: 3Gi
- PVC status capacity: 1Gi (still)
- Events show: “waiting for an external controller to expand this PVC”

Note:
- The local-path provisioner (rancher.io/local-path) does not provide a CSI external-resizer, so the capacity reported on the PV/PVC is unlikely to update despite the requested size being 3Gi. The pod mount is a hostPath-backed directory and won’t “grow” like a block device.

If you need an actually expanded filesystem with reflected capacity, use a CSI storage class that supports expansion (with an external-resizer) and migrate the PVC, or I can set that up for you.

outputSteps.content:
I updated the PVC to request 3Gi and enabled volume expansion on the StorageClass:

- Set allowVolumeExpansion: true on StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path)
- Updated PersistentVolumeClaim resize-pv/storage-pvc to requests.storage: 3Gi

Current state:
- PVC spec requests: 3Gi
- PVC status capacity: 1Gi (still)
- Events show: “waiting for an external controller to expand this PVC”

Note:
- The local-path provisioner (rancher.io/local-path) does not provide a CSI external-resizer, so the capacity reported on the PV/PVC is unlikely to update despite the requested size being 3Gi. The pod mount is a hostPath-backed directory and won’t “grow” like a block device.

If you need an actually expanded filesystem with reflected capacity, use a CSI storage class that supports expansion (with an external-resizer) and migrate the PVC, or I can set that up for you.

results.taskOutput:
The multi-tenant development environment has been created and secured as requested. Summary of what’s deployed:

Namespaces
- Created: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Labeled for policy scoping: dev namespaces have env=dev and scope=dev; dev-shared has env=dev and scope=shared; staging has env=staging and scope=restricted; prod has env=prod and scope=restricted

RBAC
- Per-developer ServiceAccounts: alice-sa (dev-alice), bob-sa (dev-bob), charlie-sa (dev-charlie)
- Full access to own namespace:
  - RoleBindings in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie bind:
    - User identities: alice, bob, charlie
    - Their respective ServiceAccounts
    - To ClusterRole admin (full namespace admin)
- Read-only to dev-shared:
  - RoleBinding in dev-shared binds:
    - Users alice, bob, charlie
    - ServiceAccounts alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa
    - To ClusterRole view (read-only)
- Staging/prod: No developer access was granted. By default, only cluster-admins (system:masters) have access.

ResourceQuotas
- dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie:
  - limits.cpu=2, limits.memory=4Gi, requests.cpu=2, requests.memory=4Gi, pods=10, services=5
- dev-shared:
  - limits.cpu=4, limits.memory=8Gi, requests.cpu=4, requests.memory=8Gi, pods=20, services=10
- staging/prod:
  - limits.cpu=8, limits.memory=16Gi, requests.cpu=8, requests.memory=16Gi, pods=50, services=20

NetworkPolicies (requires a CNI that enforces NetworkPolicy, e.g., Calico/Cilium/Antrea/Weave)
- Default deny (ingress and egress) for all created namespaces: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Allow same-namespace traffic in each namespace (ingress and egress within the namespace)
- DNS and system services:
  - Egress on TCP/UDP 53 to kube-system (CoreDNS)
  - Broad egress to kube-system allowed (system services)
- Dev namespaces can reach dev-shared:
  - In dev-* namespaces: egress allowed to namespace dev-shared
  - In dev-shared: ingress allowed from namespaces labeled env=dev, scope=dev
- Cross-developer isolation:
  - No egress from one dev namespace to another (other than dev-shared)
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - Default deny both ways; only same-namespace traffic and egress to DNS/system services are allowed

Notes and recommendations
- Users alice, bob, charlie must exist in your cluster’s auth provider with the same usernames for RBAC to apply.
- Kubernetes v1.24+: ServiceAccount tokens are not auto-created. To get a token for a developer’s SA:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice create token alice-sa
  - kubectl -n dev-bob create token bob-sa
  - kubectl -n dev-charlie create token charlie-sa
- To verify RBAC quickly:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-alice '*' '*'           -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-bob get pods           -> no
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared get pods        -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace staging get pods           -> no
- To verify network isolation:
  - Run a test pod in dev-alice and try to curl a service/pod in dev-bob (should fail)
  - Curl a service in dev-shared from dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie (should succeed)
  - From staging/prod, test that traffic to dev* namespaces is blocked; DNS queries should succeed

If you want tighter “system services” egress, we can replace the broad kube-system egress with precise policies that only allow egress to CoreDNS and any specific system endpoints you require.

---
# =========================
# Service Accounts per developer namespace
# =========================
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
# =========================
# RBAC: Full access in own namespace (admin) for user + their SA
# =========================
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-admin
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bob-admin
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: charlie-admin
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
# =========================
# RBAC: Read-only access (view) to dev-shared for users and their SAs
# =========================
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-readonly
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
# =========================
# ResourceQuotas per namespace
# =========================
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-alice-quota
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1

  name: dev-bob-quota

    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1

  name: dev-charlie-quota

    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1

  name: dev-shared-quota
  namespace: dev-shared

    requests.cpu: "4"

    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
apiVersion: v1

    pods: "50"

---

# Notes:

# - Default deny (ingress+egress) for all non-system namespaces

Steps.message:
Setting up cluster state for dev environment eval...
Setup completed successfully

The multi-tenant development environment has been created and secured as requested. Summary of what’s deployed:

Namespaces
- Created: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Labeled for policy scoping: dev namespaces have env=dev and scope=dev; dev-shared has env=dev and scope=shared; staging has env=staging and scope=restricted; prod has env=prod and scope=restricted

RBAC
- Per-developer ServiceAccounts: alice-sa (dev-alice), bob-sa (dev-bob), charlie-sa (dev-charlie)
- Full access to own namespace:
  - RoleBindings in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie bind:
    - User identities: alice, bob, charlie
    - Their respective ServiceAccounts
    - To ClusterRole admin (full namespace admin)
- Read-only to dev-shared:
  - RoleBinding in dev-shared binds:
    - Users alice, bob, charlie
    - ServiceAccounts alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa
    - To ClusterRole view (read-only)
- Staging/prod: No developer access was granted. By default, only cluster-admins (system:masters) have access.

ResourceQuotas
- dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie:
  - limits.cpu=2, limits.memory=4Gi, requests.cpu=2, requests.memory=4Gi, pods=10, services=5
- dev-shared:
  - limits.cpu=4, limits.memory=8Gi, requests.cpu=4, requests.memory=8Gi, pods=20, services=10
- staging/prod:
  - limits.cpu=8, limits.memory=16Gi, requests.cpu=8, requests.memory=16Gi, pods=50, services=20

NetworkPolicies (requires a CNI that enforces NetworkPolicy, e.g., Calico/Cilium/Antrea/Weave)
- Default deny (ingress and egress) for all created namespaces: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Allow same-namespace traffic in each namespace (ingress and egress within the namespace)
- DNS and system services:
  - Egress on TCP/UDP 53 to kube-system (CoreDNS)
  - Broad egress to kube-system allowed (system services)
- Dev namespaces can reach dev-shared:
  - In dev-* namespaces: egress allowed to namespace dev-shared
  - In dev-shared: ingress allowed from namespaces labeled env=dev, scope=dev
- Cross-developer isolation:
  - No egress from one dev namespace to another (other than dev-shared)
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - Default deny both ways; only same-namespace traffic and egress to DNS/system services are allowed

Notes and recommendations
- Users alice, bob, charlie must exist in your cluster’s auth provider with the same usernames for RBAC to apply.
- Kubernetes v1.24+: ServiceAccount tokens are not auto-created. To get a token for a developer’s SA:
  - kubectl -n dev-alice create token alice-sa
  - kubectl -n dev-bob create token bob-sa
  - kubectl -n dev-charlie create token charlie-sa
- To verify RBAC quickly:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-alice '*' '*'           -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-bob get pods           -> no
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace dev-shared get pods        -> yes
  - kubectl auth can-i --as alice --namespace staging get pods           -> no
- To verify network isolation:
  - Run a test pod in dev-alice and try to curl a service/pod in dev-bob (should fail)
  - Curl a service in dev-shared from dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie (should succeed)
  - From staging/prod, test that traffic to dev* namespaces is blocked; DNS queries should succeed

If you want tighter “system services” egress, we can replace the broad kube-system egress with precise policies that only allow egress to CoreDNS and any specific system endpoints you require.

rawInput.resource:
---
# =========================
# Service Accounts per developer namespace
# =========================
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
# =========================
# RBAC: Full access in own namespace (admin) for user + their SA
# =========================
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-admin
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bob-admin
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: charlie-admin
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
# =========================
# RBAC: Read-only access (view) to dev-shared for users and their SAs
# =========================
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-readonly
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
# =========================
# ResourceQuotas per namespace
# =========================
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-alice-quota
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-bob-quota
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-charlie-quota
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-quota
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "4"
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: staging-quota
  namespace: staging
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: prod-quota
  namespace: prod
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
# =========================
# NetworkPolicies
# Notes:
# - Requires a CNI that enforces Kubernetes NetworkPolicy (e.g., Calico, Cilium, Antrea, Weave)
# - Default deny (ingress+egress) for all non-system namespaces
# - Allow same-namespace traffic
# - Allow egress from all namespaces to DNS in kube-system (TCP/UDP 53)
# - Dev namespaces may egress to dev-shared; dev-shared ingress allows from dev namespaces
# - staging/prod isolated from dev namespaces except DNS/system egress
# =========================
# Default deny for dev-alice
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in dev-alice
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# Allow egress from dev-alice to dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: dev-shared
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# DNS egress for dev-alice
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for dev-bob
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in dev-bob
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# Allow egress from dev-bob to dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: dev-shared
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# DNS egress for dev-bob
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for dev-charlie
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in dev-charlie
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# Allow egress from dev-charlie to dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: dev-shared
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# DNS egress for dev-charlie
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# Allow ingress to dev-shared from any dev namespaces (dev-*)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-from-dev-namespaces
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: dev
              scope: dev
---
# DNS egress for dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for staging
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: staging
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in staging (optional, keep internal communication)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: staging
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# DNS egress for staging
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: staging
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for prod
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in prod (optional, keep internal communication)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# DNS egress for prod
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress

rawInput.resource:
---
# Allow egress to all kube-system services (system services) in each namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-kube-system
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-kube-system
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-kube-system
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-kube-system
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-kube-system
  namespace: staging
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-kube-system
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
  policyTypes:
    - Egress

toolCall.title: resources_create_or_update

rawInput.resource:
---
# =========================
# Service Accounts per developer namespace
# =========================
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
# =========================
# RBAC: Full access in own namespace (admin) for user + their SA
# =========================
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: alice-admin
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bob-admin
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: charlie-admin
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
# =========================
# RBAC: Read-only access (view) to dev-shared for users and their SAs
# =========================
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-readonly
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
# =========================
# ResourceQuotas per namespace
# =========================
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-alice-quota
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-bob-quota
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-charlie-quota
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "2"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    requests.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-quota
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "4"
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: staging-quota
  namespace: staging
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: prod-quota
  namespace: prod
spec:
  hard:
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
# =========================
# NetworkPolicies
# Notes:
# - Requires a CNI that enforces Kubernetes NetworkPolicy (e.g., Calico, Cilium, Antrea, Weave)
# - Default deny (ingress+egress) for all non-system namespaces
# - Allow same-namespace traffic
# - Allow egress from all namespaces to DNS in kube-system (TCP/UDP 53)
# - Dev namespaces may egress to dev-shared; dev-shared ingress allows from dev namespaces
# - staging/prod isolated from dev namespaces except DNS/system egress
# =========================
# Default deny for dev-alice
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in dev-alice
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# Allow egress from dev-alice to dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: dev-shared
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# DNS egress for dev-alice
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for dev-bob
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in dev-bob
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# Allow egress from dev-bob to dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: dev-shared
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# DNS egress for dev-bob
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for dev-charlie
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in dev-charlie
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# Allow egress from dev-charlie to dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-egress-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: dev-shared
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# DNS egress for dev-charlie
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# Allow ingress to dev-shared from any dev namespaces (dev-*)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-ingress-from-dev-namespaces
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: dev
              scope: dev
---
# DNS egress for dev-shared
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns-egress
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
---
# Default deny for staging
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: staging
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
# Allow same-namespace traffic in staging (optional, keep internal communication)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:

# DNS egress for staging
